nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich tropical reef flats and outer reef slopes from roughly 2–25 m depth; commonly found in pairs in areas of rich coral growth

decorPreferences: live rock with crevices for grazing and refuge

diet.notes: In the wild, has been reported to feed mainly on coral polyps, which makes it a partial obligate corallivore and contributes to a reputation for being difficult to wean onto captive foods. In captivity, generally accepts a varied diet of meaty preparations (mysis shrimp, finely chopped crustacean flesh, frozen marine mixes) alongside algae-based and sponge-inclusive foods offered several times daily. Newly imported specimens may initially refuse prepared foods and have been reported to benefit from live mysis or feeding-stimulant preparations during weaning.

goodWith: other peaceful, similarly sized fish-only community species
avoidWith: other Chaetodon butterflyfishes in small tanks
ornamental shrimp and other delicate invertebrates
tankmates with similar coloration in undersized systems

summary: A striking Red Sea endemic butterflyfish with diagonal black bars and a 'raccoon' eye-mask; a moderate-care fish-only species that is not reef-safe and is often considered more challenging to wean than its Indo-Pacific congener C. lunula.
careNotes: Chaetodon fasciatus is endemic to the Red Sea and the Gulf of Aden and is closely related to the more widespread Raccoon Butterflyfish (Chaetodon lunula); the two are often confused in the trade. Adults reach roughly 18–22 cm and are active swimmers, so a long, mature aquarium with plenty of swimming space and live rock for grazing and refuge is generally advised. LiveAquaria and Quality Marine both suggest a minimum of 125 US gallons (~473 L); BlueZoo Aquatics lists 75 gallons (~284 L). Given the species' adult size, territorial behavior once acclimated, and partial corallivory, a long aquarium of around 500 L is recommended to allow conspecific spacing and reduce stress-related feeding refusal. Generally considered peaceful toward heterospecifics but has been reported to become territorial as adults, particularly toward conspecifics, other Chaetodon butterflyfishes, and tankmates with similar coloration competing for food — best kept singly or as a verified mated pair, with multiples only attempted in much larger systems. Not reef-safe: FishBase reports the wild diet is mainly coral polyps, and retailers caution it will pick at corals and crustaceans. Like other Chaetodon butterflyfishes, this species is commonly considered susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when kept in poor water quality or fed an insufficiently varied, vitamin-rich, algae-inclusive diet. Wild populations are reported as monogamous, so established pairs should not be split. Captive temperature and pH ranges are averaged across LiveAquaria/Quality Marine/BlueZoo (72–78 °F, 22.2–25.6 °C); FishBase wild data is somewhat warmer (24.7–29.2 °C, mean 27.2 °C). Newly acquired specimens often refuse prepared foods for several days; targeted feeding with live or vitamin-enriched frozen mysis has been reported to help.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with monogamous mating reported as both obligate and social; forms pairs during breeding. Captive breeding of Chaetodon butterflyfishes is generally considered impractical for hobbyists due to the difficulty of rearing pelagic larvae, and there are no widely-reported captive-bred lines for this species.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for tank size (125 gal / ~473 L), temperature (72–78 °F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate), temperament, reef-safe status, and diet guidance.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Chaetodontidae / Acanthuriformes), max length (22.0 cm TL, common 15 cm), native range (Red Sea endemic, reported from Gulf of Aden), depth (2–25 m), wild temperature range (24.7–29.2 °C, mean 27.2 °C), corallivorous diet, oviparous + monogamous reproduction.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Third source confirming minimum 125 gal tank, peaceful-but-territorially-defensive temperament once acclimated, not reef-safe (picks at corals and crustaceans), and feeding regime of meaty and algae-based foods multiple times daily.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Fourth source listing a lower minimum tank size (75 gal / ~284 L) and an intermediate care level; also confirms the 'Diagonal Butterflyfish' common name. The lower tank-size estimate is included in the averaging spread; the final 500 L recommendation biases toward the cautious larger value given adult size and territoriality.
